channel.title: Comments for page "Project Proposal 2014-2112: "Dreams of Failure""
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "Project Proposal 2014-2112: "Dreams of Failure""

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
◾500 mL blood plasma from an SCP-1237-1-L-positive individual (Type O- preferred)

Slight issue with that biologically. Blood plasma is already universal regarding transfer from one individual to another. Blood typing refers to red blood cells, specifically proteins (or lack thereof) that are on the outside of the cells. The most important are the A, B, and Rh factors, hence why a normal blood type will have A, B, AB, or O (A and B absent), followed by a + or - for the Rh factor. With no red blood cells present in fresh frozen plasma, blood type doesn't matter. Most people who have a PhD (or even a BS) in a science would know that, so the issue is definitely jarring to anybody who would notice it.

EDIT: Also, the original article makes it very clear that there are likely less than 10 individuals in the entire world at any given point who are both SCP-1237-1 positive and are lucid dreamers, so the chances of finding one who is all of that that ALSO has a decently scarce blood type (2.55% of the world is O-) are pretty damn astronomical.

EDIT MKII: Blood plasma is pretty much just a solution that our other blood cells sit in. It has some pH buffers, CO2 waste, some proteins, some ions, and glucose, but that's about it. It's 95% water. No genetic material, and nothing in the original SCP-1237 article indicates that the genetic sequence that makes you an SCP-1237-1 instance codes for proteins in the plasma. There is nothing to lead me to believe that giving somebody SCP-1237-1-L plasma has any chance whatsoever of giving them the properties of such a person, even for a short time.

TL;DR: Lots of bad biology going on here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Poor bastard. He's just trying to not start a shitty cover band! LOL

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Right. What I feared from the start happened… ;>

I know nothing, or next to nothing about GOI-formats in general, and AWCY? in particular. So there is no way for me to know that this is an AWCY? document, except the tag which is unfortunately at the bottom of the document, whilst I usually prefer reading documents from the top.

Useless to say that as a consequence, this contribution to the MTF contest entry never made much sense to me from start to finish. I thought this was a project (?) submitted to the Foundation. And for a while I thought that this was the origin of ETA-11 not having much work (as stated in A Convincing Lyre): because they're a bunch of failures…

Another nitpick is that while I did consult SCP-1237's article since it was openly mentioned in the text, I did not consult the other SCPs referred - because how am I supposed to know where to jump with 'Rush' and 'Zoe'????
It would be quite nice to put some discreet but enlightening links in there!

I am going to novote on this one - I simply do not feel competent to vote any other way. Though, in view of the above… I should downvote, because my second nitpick is, I feel, justified outside of my lack of interest so far for GOI-formats.
Let's say it's a negative novote. 8-P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Two major issues with this:

  • The format makes no sense to me whatsoever. Insofar as AWCY is a cogent group of any sort, and I have doubts that they're organized to that extent in pretty much all possible interpretations, I cannot imagine them keeping standardized records like what's being written up here. Not to mention records that feel an awful lot like a mainlist article. The transition from official to casual can work in other contexts, I feel, but here it just highlights the issues with the formatting. That's probably not particularly fair to the author, but it has a pretty big impact on my reading experience.
  • I don't really care for the clear cause-and-effect relationship between the phenomenon and the artist's motivations for creating it. I feel that artwork comes from a more complex place than "I am feeling X and I therefore will Y", and this isn't really convincing me that someone out there had an experience in mind to inflict on humanity that required subverting the rules of reality. Again, this might not be fair, because I can see the role that this playing in advancing the overall narrative of the story. I appreciate that the narrative is present (there hasn't been one in some cases for other teams in this contest), and that this is moving it forward, but as a standalone piece, it's just not working for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Great stuff, I liked this when I first read it a few days ago, but now, having read CumaeanSibyl's entry, as well as SCP-1638, i like it even more.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …Okay. I see why it's confusing. Because this guy is part of the Foundation, but writing in AWCY style, so it comes off as a request for Foundation equipment. Like, through the Foundation. And that's also why I was confused as to why he's doing this. Now it makes sense, but I kind of have to wonder why the Foundation would take this guy from AWCY and not, like, do something to make sure he's not going to contact them under their noses.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I find the AWCY? format difficult to use, as it needs to tell a story while describing potential art in practical terms. It's a story about a technical description of a piece of visual art, which feels like three things that are mutually antagonistic. I personally have had an AWCY? concept for awhile and it isn't working.

This works, and uses one of the more hauntingly weird SCPs to do it. I am impressed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's how the AWCY format works. In the Abstract section, the artist explains what they're trying to do and how. In the Intent section, they explain why.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is bizarre. The first bits sound like your standard "we're gonna try magic now" ritual request, then the rest becomes personal narrative. It's like two completely different pieces of writing smashed together.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I guess O negative is universal donor for everything, including failure.

Meanwhile, I know it's in my team's interest to say this, but Smapti, I really like the changes that you've made to this since I last read it. I think it does just enough, and on reflection that is better than the over-the-top approach I suggested.

I will be upvoting this as soon as the competition is over.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is fair enough. I just always figured that mnestics were one of the most secret things in the organization, so it's hard to reconcile that with this on the whole. Hmm.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm amused by the idea that the best blood to bring about feelings of failure and futility is Type O Negative.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Where the crap would they get mnestics?

The island of Makati?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The implication is that the artist has spent several years or decades learning about the Foundation, to the point that he's aware of the identities of specific Foundation individuals, knows how their task forces are organized, and knows the designations and compositions of several Keter-class anomalies.

Chances are he's made contacts with someone on the inside aside from the late Zoe, who is capable of passing mnestics to him (along with, you know, blood plasma from a reality-bending lucid dreamer).

Alternatively, it's possible he learned how to manufacture them himself based on information he got from his sources in the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea of someone affected by 2112 burned on the fact that they can't play Rush, and want to inflict failure on the task force who had stopped that, is a very neat idea. But one thing has bothered me consistently while reading.

Where the crap would they get mnestics?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My contribution to team "String Quartet"'s MTF contest entry. This is an AWCY GOI format article incorporating elements of SCP-1237, SCP-2112, and SCP-1638.
